text: The surgeon performs bladder neck dissection during a robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy. The prostate is visible at the top of the screen with the dorsal venous complex suture ligated. Traction is applied in a cephalad direction to separate the anterior bladder neck, progressing towards the supraprostatic urethra in the midline. Careful dissection continues to identify the lateral aspect of the urethra, allowing for complete circumnavigation of the supraprostatic urethra.

The surgeon punches through the posterior tissue of the posterior bladder neck, revealing the catheter. The catheter is retracted and secured, lifting it onto the anterior abdominal wall as the bladder drops away. The dissection around the urethra leads to the division of the supraprostatic urethra, aiming to maintain a small bladder neck for facilitating anastomosis and early urinary control.

The surgeon dissects the posterior plane, revealing the midline structures including the vasa and laterally placed seminal vesicles. Hemlock clips are applied to secure the bladder wings above the neurovascular bundle. The right vas is grasped and elevated, followed by anterior retraction. Using sharp and blunt dissection, the right seminal vesicle is identified and mobilized down to its tip.

The ProGrasp instrument is used to grasp the tip of the right seminal vesicle, and a hemlock clip is applied around the right vas deferens and the vasal artery located posteriorly. An additional artery heading towards the tip of the seminal vesicle is controlled with another hemlock clip. The same procedure is performed on the left side, where the ProGrasp elevates the left vas deferens towards the anterior abdominal wall. The left seminal vesicle is exposed and elevated using monopolar scissors and bipolar instruments, facilitating the clipping and division of the left vas deferens. Further retraction is achieved, and a clip is applied to secure the artery at the tip of the left seminal vesicle.

The surgeon mobilizes the end of the seminal vesicle and retracts it towards the anterior abdominal wall, exposing Denonvilliers' fascia, a biolaminar structure separating the anterior rectal wall from the posterior aspect of the prostate.
